conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me ask you some questions to assess your eligibility.

First, do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have one child, Alex, who's 6 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have one child, Alex, who is 6 years old.

Now I need to ask you: Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't actually. I live abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since you live abroad, there are specific circumstances where you may still be able to claim Child Benefit.

Could you tell me which country you're currently living in, and whether you are a Crown servant (for example, working for the UK government or armed forces overseas)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not a Crown servant, no. I'm just living abroad - not working for the UK government or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. 

Can I also ask - do you have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or are you an EU/EEA or Swiss national? And are you or have you previously been working in the UK or paying UK National Insurance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. I'm not an EU, EEA or Swiss national either. And I'm not currently working in the UK or paying UK National Insurance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. 

Just to confirm - are you a British citizen, and if so, when did you move abroad? Also, is Alex living with you abroad, or is Alex living in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living with me abroad, yes. We're together here.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that Alex is living with you abroad.

Based on the information you've provided, I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me summarize what you've told me:
- You have one child, Alex, who is 6 years old
- You live abroad (not in the UK)
- Alex lives with you abroad
- You are not a Crown servant
- You do not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- You are not an EU/EEA or Swiss national
- You are not currently working in the UK or paying UK National Insurance

According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 **and you live in the UK**. While there are exceptions for people living abroad, these apply to:
- Crown servants (which you are not)
- People living in certain specific countries with reciprocal agreements
- People with settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (which you don't have)
- EU/EEA or Swiss nationals meeting certain conditions (which doesn't apply to you)

Since you and Alex are both living abroad and none of the exceptions apply to your situation, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry, but based on your circumstances, you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

The main issue is that you and Alex are living abroad, and you don't meet any of the exceptions that would allow someone living outside the UK to claim Child Benefit. The UK residency requirement is a fundamental condition for eligibility, and unfortunately, your situation doesn't fall within the specific categories (such as Crown servants or those with certain EU settlement statuses) that are exempt from this requirement.

If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if you return to live in the UK with Alex - you would be able to apply for Child Benefit at that time.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16) and lives with the claimant, which would normally meet the age and responsibility requirements. However, both the claimant and Alex live abroad (outside the UK). The claimant is not a Crown servant, does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, is not an EU/EEA or Swiss national, and is not working in the UK or paying UK National Insurance. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 AND you live in the UK. The exceptions for living abroad do not apply to this claimant's circumstances. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 6), and both live abroad. While Alex meets the age requirement (under 16) and the claimant is responsible for Alex, the residency requirement is not met. The claimant does not fall under any of the exceptions that allow people living abroad to claim Child Benefit (Crown servant status, EU Settlement Scheme, or working/paying UK National Insurance). Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
